html:
Axel Fredrik Cronstedt, born December 23, 1722, in Ströppsta, Turinge parish, Stockholm County, and died August 19, 1765, in Säter, was a Swedish mining inspector, mineralogist, and chemist. He is considered the founder of modern mineralogy and geology, and he discovered the element nickel.

Axel Fredrik Cronstedt was born in 1722 as one of 25 children to Gabriel Olderman, who was adopted into the noble Cronstedt family, and his second wife, Maria Elisabeth Adlerberg, a descendant of Archbishop Olaus Swebilius.

In 1738, Cronstedt enrolled at Uppsala University, initially studying mathematics with the intention, according to his father's wishes, of becoming a military officer. However, lectures in mineralogy given by Johan Gottschalk Wallerius sparked his interest in this subject, which he then devoted himself to wholeheartedly. In 1742, he began working as an auditor at the Bergskollegium (Mining Authority), where he became an extra ordinary notary in 1746 and a regular notary in 1747. In 1747, he was appointed as a sworn expert in the Öster- and Västerbergslagen mining districts, and in 1758, he became the mining inspector for those districts. In 1753, he was elected a member of the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences. Cronstedt's valuable mineral collection was purchased by the Danish landowner Tönnes Christian Bruun-Neergaard.

==Cronstedt and Modern Geology==

Cronstedt is perhaps best known for his groundbreaking studies of minerals and rocks, which were epoch-making. Like in botany and zoology, both Carl von Linné and Wallerius attempted to systematically classify the mineral and rock kingdom. Linné, with his strong sense of form, placed great importance on the crystalline properties of minerals, inspiring Jean-Baptiste Romé de l'Isles' pioneering work (1772), and Wallerius' "Mineralogica" (1747) was the first true handbook in this field.

In many respects, Linné's approach was not suitable for studying minerals and rocks. He focused too narrowly on their external characteristics, such as color, structure, variations in form, etc. While these characteristics are indicative of different species in animals and plants, this is not applicable to minerals and rocks. Neither Linné nor Wallerius distinguished between simple minerals and rocks, between true rocks, "petrified remains" (fossils), peculiar rock formations (giant potholes, grooved stones, "eagle stones," "mathematical stones"), or "stone creations" (pearls, rooster stones, tire, stones of flying dragons, etc.).

Cronstedt removed the latter items from mineralogy and drew a distinction between the rock kingdom and the animal kingdom. He laid the foundation for modern geology by differentiating between minerals and rocks, in his anonymously published work "Försök til mineralogie, eller mineral-rikets upställning" (1758), which was translated into Danish (two different translations), German (three translations, 1760, 1770, and 1780), French (1771), English (1770-1772, 1781, and 1788), and likely into several other languages.

The reform that Cronstedt implemented in mineralogy provoked resistance and conflict during his time, largely because Cronstedt did not focus on the external appearance of minerals but considered their chemical properties to be more important for classification. He was the first to use a blowpipe, with which he made many discoveries. Cronstedt completely distinguished between cobalt minerals that were morphologically similar but chemically entirely different: cobaltglanz, speiskobolt, and linnéite. He described the copper ore kuban, which was mistaken for copper pyrite for nearly a century.

The water-containing silicates, known as zeolites, had been completely overlooked by mineralogists. However, Cronstedt recognized and described a zeolite from Åreskutan as a distinct mineral, which later became representative of the large group of zeolites.

== The Discovery of Nickel ==

The discovery of the element nickel occurred at the Los mine in Hälsingland. Georg Brandt, a mining official, had already recognized this metal-like substance as a distinct element in 1735. Henric Kalmeter had begun mining cobalt and bismuth, but the cobalt extracted did not produce a pure blue color. Cronstedt then initiated an investigation into the cause and discovered that the ore contained only a small amount of cobalt. Instead, it contained a larger quantity of a new metal, which he named nickel, and described in "Observations and experiments made with an ore from the Los cobalt mines" (Proceedings of the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences 1751-54). Although many of nickel's reactions are highly characteristic, the discovery was long disputed by contemporary foreign chemists, for example, René Just Haüy still doubted it in 1801.

Cronstedt was also the first to draw attention to "tungsten" in 1751, a mineral that was then believed to be an iron ore. The "tungsten" from Bispbergs klack in Dalarna and from the Bastnäs mines in Västmanland were later found to be different minerals: scheelite and cerite, respectively. It was likely in scheelite from Bispberg that Scheele first described a tungsten oxide as a new substance, and in mineral samples from the Bastnäs mines that Jacob Berzelius and Hisinger, as well as independently Martin Klaproth in 1803, discovered the metal cerium.

How certain are we about this?

Information about individuals from the Age of Liberty is well-documented through parliamentary records, letters, and archives. However, interpretations of their roles are often influenced by later political biases.
tldr: Axel Fredrik Cronstedt was a Swedish scientist who laid the foundation for modern geology, the study of rocks and the Earth's composition. He is also known for discovering the metal nickel.
lattlast:
Axel Fredrik Cronstedt was born in 1722 and became a highly significant researcher in chemistry and mineralogy, the study of minerals and rocks. He studied at Uppsala University and later worked for the Bergskollegium, an authority responsible for mining and metals. He was also a member of the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences.

Cronstedt revolutionized how scientists viewed rocks by focusing on their chemical properties rather than just their external appearance. By doing so, he laid the foundation for modern geology, the science of the Earth's structure. He wrote an important book on the subject that was translated into many languages.

One of his greatest achievements was discovering the metal nickel in a mine in Hälsingland. He was able to explain why other researchers had previously mistaken it for other metals. He also made significant discoveries regarding other elements, such as tungsten and cerium.
